coredata.dc:title: Super-low friction and wear in steel contacts enabled by tribo-induced structural degradation of graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Reducing friction is a promising strategy to decrease material losses and energy consumption in industrial systems. However, in aqueous-lubricated steel contacts, the contact pressure rarely exceeds 50 MPa during super-low friction due to excessive wear. This work demonstrates that even in steel/steel contacts, by combining graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with aqueous glycerol, it is possible to maintain super-low friction (µ ≈ 0.012) under a contact pressure as high as 316.5 MPa. Moreover, the use of GQDs improved the wear performance by 98 % compared to pure aqueous glycerol due to the formation of a tribochemical film, resulting from the electrostatic adsorption of GQDs on the positively charged sites on the worn surface. In particular, the exfoliation of graphene sheets within GQDs, the shearing of graphene layers inside the GQDs, and the OH–OH repulsion between the asperities shortens the running-in period and consequently reduces the friction and wear. At the same time, the formation of a chemically adsorbed tribofilm containing friction-induced structurally degraded GQDs protects the surface from wear and facilitates the maintenance of super-low friction at high contact pressures by improving the load-carrying capacity. This study suggests that green nano-lubricants based on GQDs have immense potential in sustainable engineering.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of GQDs. (a) typical HRTEM image and (b) statistical analysis of the lateral size of the GQDs determined by TEM. (c) AFM image and topographic height profiles of GQDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of GQDs. (e) XPS survey spectra of GQDs and (f) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of GQDs.

Evaluation of the dispersion stability of GQDs in aqueous glycerol over a period of time. (a) UV–vis spectrum of GQDs (inset showing the GQDs in aqueous glycerol, (i) under normal light and (ii) under UV light at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm). (b) Linear calibration curve obtained by measuring the absorbance values at 340 nm for GQDsaq immediately after sonication. (c) Change in relative concentration against the number of hours for 0.05, 0.075, and 0.1 wt% GQDs, respectively.

Evaluation of the tribological performance of GQDs in aqueous glycerol. (a) Average COF and WSD values after the friction test performed with water, aqueous glycerol, GNPsaq, and GQDsaq (3.5 N, 100 mm/s, 75 min, and 0.1 wt% for GQDs and GNPs). (b) Variation of COF with different concentrations of GQDsaq against sliding time (3.5 N, 100 mm/s, 75 min). Variation of average COF and MCP (c) with different concentrations of GQDs (3.5 N, 100 mm/s, 75 min), (d) at different loads (0.1 wt% GQDsaq, 100 mm/s, 75 min), (e) at different sliding speeds (0.1 wt% GQDsaq, 3.5 N, 75 min). (f) Variation in the wear volume with all tribological parameters.

Wear behavior of steel ball lubricated with different concentrations of GQDsaq. Optical micrographs and 3D white-light interferometry of the wear scars obtained after 75 min friction test (3.5 N, 100 mm/s) lubricated with (a) 0.05 wt%, (b) 0.075 wt%, (c) 0.1 wt%, and (d) 0.15 wt% of GQDsaq, respectively.

Characterization of the wear track lubricated with 0.1 wt% of GQDsaq (test conditions: 3.5 N and 100 mm/s). (a) SEM image showing the wear track with a width of around 120 µm, (b) high-magnification SEM image implying the tribofilm formation inside the wear track; (c) White light interferometry of the wear scar along with 2D profile indicating negligible wear on steel disc with a maximum wear depth of around 80 nm; (d) Raman spectrum obtained inside the wear track at a location marked in Fig. 5b.

XPS characterization on the disc’s wear track lubricated with 0.1 wt% of GQDsaq (test conditions: 3.5 N and 100 mm/s). High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, and (c) Fe 2p inside the worn surface.

Cross-sectional TEM analysis of the tribofilm formed on the worn steel surface with the incorporation of 0.1 wt% GQDsaq as a lubricant (3.5 N, 100 mm/s, 75 min). (a) STEM bright-field image of the tribofilm, indicating a thickness ranging from 8.2 to 35.2 nm, (b) STEM HAADF image of the carbon-rich tribofilm, (c) HRTEM image of the selected area marked with “c” in Fig. 7b showing the multilayered structure of GQDs (inset showing the FFT analysis of the crystalline features corresponding to 0.24 nm of interlayer spacing), (d) EELS spectrum of the tribofilm marked with “d” in Fig. 7b showing the existence of Fe and O in the tribofilm along with C (inset showing the EELS spectrum of C K-edge), (e) EDS elemental mapping of the selected area marked with “e” in Fig. 7a containing Cr cap layer, tribofilm and steel substrate.

Proposed lubrication mechanism of the self-mated steel contacts in the boundary-lubrication regime using GQDsaq. (a) Overall representation of tribotest (inset SEM image of the wear track), (b) schematic of the tribochemical film formed on the worn steel surface, (c) adsorption mechanism of the GQDs indicating the anion from the GQDs adsorbed onto the positively charged worn steel surface via electrostatic interaction, facilitating the formation of a robust tribofilm, (d) schematic of the OH–OH repulsion between asperities preventing direct contact.

head.ce:title: Super-low friction and wear in steel contacts enabled by tribo-induced structural degradation of graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reducing friction is a promising strategy to decrease material losses and energy consumption in industrial systems. However, in aqueous-lubricated steel contacts, the contact pressure rarely exceeds 50 MPa during super-low friction due to excessive wear. This work demonstrates that even in steel/steel contacts, by combining graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with aqueous glycerol, it is possible to maintain super-low friction (µ ≈ 0.012) under a contact pressure as high as 316.5 MPa. Moreover, the use of GQDs improved the wear performance by 98 % compared to pure aqueous glycerol due to the formation of a tribochemical film, resulting from the electrostatic adsorption of GQDs on the positively charged sites on the worn surface. In particular, the exfoliation of graphene sheets within GQDs, the shearing of graphene layers inside the GQDs, and the OH–OH repulsion between the asperities shortens the running-in period and consequently reduces the friction and wear. At the same time, the formation of a chemically adsorbed tribofilm containing friction-induced structurally degraded GQDs protects the surface from wear and facilitates the maintenance of super-low friction at high contact pressures by improving the load-carrying capacity. This study suggests that green nano-lubricants based on GQDs have immense potential in sustainable engineering.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear are leading causes of wasted energy and failed components in mechanical systems involving relative motion. It is reported that 80 % of mechanical components fail due to wear, and 30 % of the world's primary energy is consumed by friction [1–3]. In light of global sustainable-development goals, there is a need to develop energy-efficient systems to reduce carbon footprint and energy consumption [4]. One of the most-studied methods for reducing friction and wear is the use of liquid lubricants, such as water-based lubricants [5–9], oil-based lubricants [10–18], and lubricants in combination with nano-additives [19–26]. However, traditional lubricants that are used to mitigate friction and wear contain hazardous elements such as phosphorus, zinc, and sulfur [27], which can contribute to environmental pollution. Consequently, international regulations have become more stringent regarding the use of such lubricant additives [27–29]. In contrast, apart from biodegradable oils [28], various polyhydric alcohols, for example, ethanediol, glycerol, 1,3-propylene glycol, and myo-inositol, are gaining attention as green lubricants due to their environmental friendliness and high water solubility [21]. Glycerol is the most widely used, due to its good biocompatibility and cost-effectiveness [30]. Moreover, several tribological studies reported super-low friction in various tribo-systems using polyhydric alcohols [5,8,31,32]. However, in most of these studies, a long running-in period is required to achieve stable, super-low friction, which leads to a mechanical deterioration of the tribo-pairs and, consequently, severe wear.

Therefore, various researchers have attempted to achieve super-low friction by incorporating nanoparticles into polyhydric alcohols, with most studies focusing on a uni-directional sliding motion [21–23,33]. However, the actual contact pressure during the stages of super-low friction is normally low, despite the high initial contact pressure (i.e., 0.7–1.0 GPa). This is due to either the mechanical action or the tribochemical reactions that occur during the running-in phase. In aqueous-lubricated conditions, super-low friction is typically realized at a low contact pressure between a self-mated steel contact [21,26], or it only appears in certain tribopairs like steel/ta-c [8], Si3N4/sapphire [6,22,24,25,33,34], and self-mated DLC [35]. In a study by Chen et al. [21], superlubricity was achieved in a mixed-lubrication regime between a steel/steel contact by employing 0.01 wt% nano-diamonds in aqueous glycerol. Although these nano-diamonds improve the anti-wear performance relative to aqueous glycerol, the contact pressure decreased to about 50 MPa during the superlubricity period, which is substantially lower than the initial contact pressure (i.e., 667.5 MPa).

Therefore, achieving near-zero friction in a steel/steel contact at a high contact pressure remains challenging. In recent years, very few studies have reported on superlubricity between self-mated steel contacts using aqueous lubricants. However, a long running-in period leads to severe wear of the tribopairs, due to which the contact pressures during the super-lubricated state in these studies rarely exceed 50 MPa [21,26]. Therefore, it is desirable to achieve super-low friction between the friction pairs of common industrial materials, such as bearing steel under a high contact pressure.

Over the past decade, extensive research has been conducted to investigate the tribological performance of graphene-based liquid lubricants due to their superior frictional and anti-wear properties [22,30,36,37]. Ge et al. reported superlubricity between Si3N4/sapphire tribopairs resulting from the synergistic effect of ethanediol and graphene oxide (GO) at a contact pressure of 111 MPa [22]. Despite the excellent mechanical and tribological properties of graphene-based lubricants, certain factors limit the prospects for their commercialization, such as the embedding stability between the micro-asperities and the nanoscale convex points of the rubbing surfaces due to their large size in the lateral direction [20,38], the restricted interlayer slippage [36,39], the limited time of the superlubricity [1], and their tendency to agglomerate in conventional lubricating oils [40,41].

Carbon-based quantum dots, especially graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have recently attracted the attention of scientists due to their favorable physicochemical properties, limited cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, uniform size distribution, and tunable functionalized surfaces [42]. Importantly, GQDs exhibit an even lower toxicity than GO [43]. Unlike carbon quantum dots, GQDs are regarded as smaller iterations of GO, featuring a graphene structure enclosed within the dots, contributing to their exceptional physical properties [44]. Recently, carbon dots were found to be a better substitute for graphene and GO-based aqueous lubricants owing to their superior dispersion stability and their ability to easily penetrate into the contact surfaces [20,45–48]. Zeng et al. [45] demonstrated that the GQDs as an additive in polyethylene glycol improved the wear performance by 40.5 %, which is due to the formation of a dense disordered carbon film on the sliding interface. Additionally, due to their fluorescent properties, they have a potential application prospect in non-destructive oil leakage detection, lubrication regulation, and monitoring [45,49]. A recent study involving a Si3N4/sapphire tribopair [33] showed that incorporating quantum dots of graphene oxide (GOGQDs) into an aqueous ethanediol solution provides superlubricity and significantly improves the wear performance by 90 % due to the formation of a tribofilm containing GOGQDs. Several lubrication mechanisms, such as the rolling effect, the formation of a protective tribofilm, shear sliding within the lattice layers of carbon quantum dots, and frictional-induced structural transformation, are believed to be responsible for their improved lubrication performance [20,33,50]. However, the primary lubrication mechanism of GQDs as nano-additives in liquid-based lubricants for self-mated steel contacts remains unclear, as there is limited research on this topic.

Here, we explored the tribological performance of GQDs as an additive in aqueous glycerol for a steel/steel contact on the macroscale. Moreover, we investigated the influence of the GQDs’ concentration, the contact pressure, and the sliding speed on their tribological performance. The worn surfaces were characterized by an optical microscope, a 3D white-light interferometer, and a scanning electron microscope. Furthermore, the tribofilm on the steel surface was investigated using Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope equipped with electron-energy-loss spectroscopy.

Glycerol with a purity of more than 99.5 %, derived from renewable feedstock, was acquired from Merck, Germany. The blue luminescent GQDs were prepared by pyrolyzing citric acid and provided by XFNANO (Nanjing, CN). The graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) used for comparison were acquired from Graphene Supermarket (Graphene Laboratories Inc., Calverton, USA). The GNPs had a lateral dimension of 2–8 µm and were comprised of 3–8 graphene monolayers with a thickness of less than 3 nm, as specified by the supplier. The glycerol aqueous solution was prepared by mixing demineralized water (10 wt%) and pure glycerol (90 wt%), followed by stirring (WiseStir MSH-20D, DE) for 1 h at room temperature to ensure homogenization. The addition of 10 wt% demineralized water to glycerol lowered the viscosity by ∼82 % (i.e., 131.5 mm2/s) compared to pure glycerol (i.e., 720.1 mm2/s), which is much more relevant viscosity for many applications, as well as enable achieving boundary-lubrication conditions. In addition, the freezing point of the mixture decreased to −1˚C, which further extends its application [51]. The GQD aqueous solutions (GQDsaq) were prepared by adding GQDs to the glycerol aqueous solution to a concentration of 0.05, 0.075, 0.1 and 0.15 wt%. Then, the mixtures were ultrasonicated using a bath sonicator (Sonis 4 GT, Entjernej, SI) to ensure homogeneity.

The microstructure and morphology of the GQDs were characterized by a probe Cs-corrected scanning transmission electron microscope (JEOL, ARM 200 CF, JP) equipped with Gatan DualEEELS Quantum ER spectrometer and an atomic force microscope (AFM-Asylum research MFP 3D origin, UK). An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; PHI-TFA, Physical Electronics Inc, USA) was used to analyze the chemical structure of the GQDs, while an attenuated total-reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (ATR-FTIR; Perkin Elmer instruments, USA) was employed to investigate the surface functional groups. The structural units of the GQDs were determined using a confocal Raman microscope (XploRAPLUS, HORIBA, JP). The ultraviolet–visible spectrums of GQDsaq were acquired using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hewlett-Packard-8453, Palo Alto, CA). Concurrently, the structure of the GQDs was further verified by revealing the light-excitation property of the GQDs using a custom-made, 100-W, ultraviolet lamp with a 365-nm wavelength. The surface charge of the GQDs was determined by measuring the zeta (ζ) potential (Zetasizer NanoZS, Malvern, UK) using a 0.1-mg/mL dispersion of GQDs in absolute ethanol as a solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the long-term stability of GQDs in aqueous glycerol
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis spectra were collected to investigate the long-term dispersion stability of GQDs in aqueous glycerol. A linear calibration curve was constructed to establish the relationship between the absorbance and the concentration by determining the absorbance value at a specific wavelength of 340 nm for each concentration of GQDsaq immediately after sonication, based on the Lambert-Beer law, which states that absorbance is proportional to concentration [52]. Subsequently, the absorption spectra were acquired for up to 144 h (6 days) at wavelengths ranging from 200 to 800 nm. The relative concentrations of each GQDsaq were then plotted against time to assess their dispersion stability.

The macroscale tribological tests were conducted in a ball-on-flat-disc configuration using a universal micro-tribometer (UMT-2, Bruker, USA) in reciprocating mode. All tests were performed at room temperature, 26 ± 2 °C, with a relative humidity of 28–32 % and repeated three to five times to confirm reproducibility. The friction pairs were made of AISI 52100 steel with a surface roughness (Rq) of 40 nm, where the diameter of the ball was 10 mm. Before testing, the balls and discs were cleaned in ethanol and then acetone (10 min with each reagent). For each test, the GQDsaq suspension was carefully introduced between the friction pairs with a volume of 50 µL. Initially, the tests were performed with different GQD concentrations (i.e., 0.05, 0.075, 0.1, and 0.15 wt%) at a normal load of 3.5 N and a sliding speed of 100 mm/s to determine the optimum concentration for better tribological performance. These parameters were carefully chosen based on the existing literature for super-low friction involving aqueous lubricants and nanoparticles [21,26,38]. Furthermore, the tribological performance of pure water, aqueous glycerol, and GNPs-containing aqueous glycerol (GNPsaq, 0.1 wt%) was also evaluated. The objective was to investigate the effectiveness of the GQDsaq compared to commonly used aqueous lubricants. After determining the optimal concentration of GQDs, the subsequent tribological tests were carried out at normal applied loads of 2, 3.5, 5 and 10 N, corresponding to maximum Hertzian contact pressures of 600, 700, 800, and 1000 MPa, respectively. Finally, sliding speeds were also varied from 50, 100, 150, and 200 mm/s by performing tests at the optimum load and GQD concentration. These experimental conditions were chosen to ensure boundary lubrication (see Note S1).

After the tribological tests, both worn surfaces were carefully cleaned with isopropanol to remove any lubricant residue on their surfaces. Subsequently, the wear-scar diameter (WSD) and roughness were measured using 3D white-light interferometry (Bruker – Contour GT-K0, DE). The wear-track surface on the disc was almost free of wear; therefore, the wear volume of the balls having an effective WSD after the tribological tests was estimated using the following equation [53]:

In addition, the topography of the worn surfaces was analyzed in a high vacuum using a scanning electron microscope (SEM; JEOL, JSM IT100, JP), while an energy-dispersive spectrometer (EDS) was used to analyze the elemental distribution. The chemical nature of the wear scar on the disc was analyzed using XPS, whereas the Raman spectra on the wear tracks were recorded using a high-performance confocal Raman microscope (XploRAPLUS, HORIBA, JP) at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. A TEM lamella was prepared from the worn surface using an in-situ lift-out technique by a focused-ion-beam (FIB)-SEM dual system (Heliosnanolab650, FEI, NL) to investigate the tribo-induced interfacial changes. A 30-nm-thick Cr layer was first deposited onto the surface, followed by a Pt layer to protect the tribofilm during the subsequent preparation of the lamella. The lamella was subjected to a cross-sectional examination using HRTEM and scanning TEM (STEM), operating at an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. Finally, electron-energy-loss spectroscopy (EELS) was used to analyze the bonding and compositional characteristics of the tribofilm at a high spatial resolution. The TEM/STEM images and EELS spectra were analyzed using Digital Micrograph software (Gatan Inc., CA, USA).

The chemical and physical properties of the GQDs are of great significance for their tribological performance. The microstructural characteristics of the GQDs show that they are close to spherical in shape and uniform in size (Fig. 1 a). Fig. S1a showed that they possess an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (1120) plane of graphene [54,55]. The average particle size of the GQDs is 2.5 ± 0.3 nm, as determined by TEM (Fig. 1b). The topographic height profiles obtained from the AFM image of the GQDs show that the thickness of the GQDs is in the range 1–2 nm (Fig. 1c), indicating that the GQDs have a bilayer or monolayer structure [33]. The results in Fig. 1b and 1c indicate that the GQDs are anisotropic, with lateral dimensions larger than the height [56,57]. The Raman spectrum of the GQDs showed characteristic D and G bands at 1345 cm−1 and 1553 cm−1, respectively, with an ID /IG ratio of 0.98 (Fig. S1b). The D band arises due to structural imperfections such as disruption in graphitic structure, broken edges, and sp3-like discontinuities, whereas the G band represents the sp2 hybridized structure of the GQDs [58,59]. The appearance of a broad, higher-order, 2D band is a characteristic feature of a graphene structure with a few layers [39]. The absence of a doublet between the D and G bands and a broad 2D peak indicates that the GQDs have a nanocrystalline graphitic structure [60]. ATR-FTIR spectroscopy was employed to study the surface functional groups. The transmittance FTIR spectra of the GQDs indicate the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl, and carbonyl on the edges and in the basal plane of the GQDs (Fig. 1d). The IR peaks observed at 1687 and 1402 cm−1 correspond to the C=O and C–O vibrations of the carboxyl groups [61,62], while the peaks at 1213 and 1106 cm−1 were observed due to C–O stretching of the hydroxyl groups. The weak shoulder at 1650 cm−1 corresponds to the C=C vibrations of the sp2 hybridized carbon core [63]. In addition, a strong absorption peak at 1283 cm−1 and a weak peak at 1330 cm−1 indicate the presence of C–O–C and C–H bands, respectively. XPS was carried out to further confirm the presence of functional groups in the GQD powder. The survey spectrum in Fig. 1e shows that the C concentration is 55 at. %, and the oxygen concentration is approximately 45 at. %, with a C/O ratio of 1.20 (Fig. 1e). Further, a C 1s spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1f) is deconvoluted into four components, corresponding to sp2 C=C at a binding energy of 284.3 eV, sp3 C–C/C–H at 285.2 eV, C–O (C–OH/C–O–C) at 286.9 eV, and a C=O bond of carbonyl and carboxyl groups at a binding energy of 288.7 eV [64]. In addition, the peak position and shape of the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2) further support the C 1s fitting results, indicating that most of the oxygen was bonded to carbon in the form of C–O [64]. The results show that the surface components identified by XPS are consistent with the FTIR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uv–vis and investigation of dispersion stability
ce:section.ce:para: The dispersion stability of the GQDs in aqueous glycerol was further characterized by a UV–vis spectroscopic analysis. The obtained UV–vis absorption spectra showed an absorption peak at a wavelength of about 340 nm for the GQDs, which originates from the n–π* transition of the carbonyl groups (C=O) that are usually present at the edges or in the carbogenic core (Fig. 2 a) [54]. This implies that the UV absorption in these GQDs is primarily related to these oxygenated surface states (C=O). The GQDs demonstrate a bright-blue emission under UV light of 365 nm, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 2a.

The absorption spectra of the GQDs in aqueous glycerol at various concentrations, recorded immediately after the bath sonication, are presented in Fig. 2a. As the Lambert-Beer law suggests, the absorption increases with an increase in the GQD concentration [52]. Therefore, a linear calibration curve was constructed by measuring the absorbance value at a specific wavelength of 340 nm for each concentration of GQDsaq immediately after sonication (Fig. 2b). The absorbance at 340 nm was chosen as an indicator to reflect the change in the concentration of the GQDs with the storage time. Using this linear relationship, the relative concentration was measured as a function of sedimentation time, as shown in Fig. 2c. It can be seen that the relative concentration decreases slightly in the first 24 to 36 h, since the behavior of the nanofluid is affected by various forces such as the gravitational, electrostatic, and van der Waals [65]. Once the equilibrium between these forces is established, there is no significant loss in the relative concentration, and it remains stable for 144 h (6 days), indicating the excellent dispersibility of the GQDs in aqueous glycerol.

The friction and wear performance of the GQDsaq was evaluated at the steel/steel interface with different GQD concentrations, normal loads, and sliding speeds (Fig. 3 ). Fig. 3a shows the significance of the GQDsaq over various aqueous lubricants (conditions: 3.5 N, 100 mm/s, 75 min, and 0.1 wt% for GQDs and GNPs). The results show that super-low friction is achieved between the surfaces lubricated with GQDsaq, with an average coefficient of friction (COF) of 0.012, approaching superlubricity. In contrast, the COFs of pure water and aqueous glycerol are much higher, indicating their inability to form an effective lubricating film. The GNPsaq significantly reduces the COF by 94 % and 82.5 % compared with pure water and aqueous glycerol, respectively, but it is still 33 % higher than the GQDsaq.

The GQDsaq shows superior anti-wear performance compared to the surfaces lubricated with pure water, aqueous glycerol, and GNPsaq (Fig. 3a). The average WSD lubricated with GQDsaq is ∼ 117 µm, much smaller than the corresponding WSD of the surfaces lubricated with pure water (∼1426 µm), aqueous glycerol (∼312 µm), and GNPsaq (∼213 µm). The increase in WSD from the Hertzian contact diameter (i.e., 96 µm) is only about 22 % for the GQDsaq. In contrast, for water, aqueous glycerol, and GNPsaq, it is about 1385 %, 225 %, and 123 %, respectively.

Fig. 3b demonstrates the effect of GQD concentration on the friction behavior. The initial baseline experiment using pure aqueous glycerol shows that the COF decreases to 0.067 in the first 650 sec, then gradually increases to 0.10, and fluctuates between 0.10 and 0.14. In contrast, adding even a small amount of GQDs (i.e., 0.05 wt%) resulted in a significant reduction in COF compared with the aqueous glycerol. Increasing the concentration of GQDs from 0.05 to 0.1 wt% shortens the running-in time and brings the contact system closer to superlubricity, with a COF reduction from 0.019 to 0.010. On the other hand, the COF increases to 0.020 as the concentration of GQDs increases to 0.15 wt%, as shown in Fig. 3b.

Fig. 3c shows the influence of the GQD concentration on the real contact pressure during super-low friction; this real measured contact pressure (MCP) is determined by dividing the applied normal load by the worn area on the ball (the contact area). The MCP increases with increasing concentration from 0.05 to 0.1 wt%, reaching a maximum value of 316.5 MPa, which means 8.5-times higher than aqueous glycerol (i.e., 36.8 MPa). However, a further increase of the concentration to 0.15 wt% GQDs decreases the MCP to 252.8 MPa. Based on the results presented in Fig. 3b and 3c, we concluded that 0.1 wt% is the optimum concentration for GQDs to achieve super-low friction at a higher contact pressure. Therefore, 0.1 wt% was subsequently used to investigate the effects of the applied normal loads.

Fig. 3d shows the variation of COF and MCP as a function of applied normal loads. This was investigated by performing tests with a 0.1 wt% GQDsaq lubricant at a sliding speed of 100 mm/s. The average COF decreases from ∼0.022 to 0.012, approaching superlubricity when increasing the applied normal load from 2 to 3.5 N. At the same time, the corresponding MCP during the stable super-low friction increases from 220 to 316.5 MPa. In contrast, the COF increases to 0.037 at a normal applied load of 5 N, and the corresponding MCP decreases to 297 MPa, while it increases to 427 MPa under a 10-N applied load. It should be noted that the MCP does not increase linearly with the applied normal load. The initial Hertzian contact pressure (HCP) between the self-mated steel contacts increases monotonically from 600 to 1000 MPa when increasing the load from 2 to 10 N, which could affect the wear behavior during the running-in phase. However, the variation trends of the MCP are different from the HCP, where the HCP represents only the initial contact pressure, and the MCP is always much lower than the HCP due to the inevitable wear of the ball during the friction test. Therefore, the wear of the steel ball increases with the normal load, and likewise the MCP does not always increase in a monotonic fashion with the applied normal load. Notably, the contact pressure is always greater than 200 MPa during the super-low friction, which is rarely observed for steel/steel contacts in reciprocating motion, implying the excellent anti-wear performance of the GQDsaq. Based on these findings, it can be concluded that the 3.5 N load, which corresponds to an HCP of 700 MPa, is the optimum value for GQDsaq to achieve super-low friction at a high contact pressure. This load was thus used to investigate the additional effect of the sliding speed.

Fig. 3e indicates the effect of sliding speed on the average COFs and contact pressure when lubricated with 0.1 wt% GQDsaq under a normal load of 3.5 N. The results indicate that the average COF decreases from ∼0.02 to 0.012 with an increase in the sliding speed from 50 to 100 mm/s. Vice-versa, the MCP increases from 240 to 316.5 MPa. However, as the sliding speed increases from 150 to 200 mm/s, the average COF increases from 0.018 to 0.023, while the MCP fluctuates in the range ∼228–240 MPa.

The influence of the contact conditions and the concentration of the GQDsaq on the wear performance is summarized in Fig. 3f. The wear volume decreases by 37 % as the concentration of GQDsaq increases from 0.05 to 0.1 wt%, reaching a minimum value of 1.86×10-6 mm3, which is 98 % less than the surface lubricated with aqueous glycerol. Furthermore, it increases with the applied normal load, reaching a maximum value of 8.62×10-6 mm3, which is still much lower for self-mated steel contacts. In contrast, the variation in sliding speed does not significantly affect the wear volume, which lies between 1.86×10-6 and 3.62×10-6 mm3. Table S2 and Fig. S4 show the average WSDs of the balls tested under different contact conditions, further confirming the results shown in Fig. 3f.

The results in Fig. 3 imply that this realization of excellent friction and wear performance is due to a specific lubrication mechanism rather than elasto-hydrodynamic lubrication. The advantages of GQDs as lubricant additives over other aqueous lubricants and 2D graphitic materials for self-mated steel contacts are summarized as follows: (i) GQDs have a higher dispersion stability and uniformity; (ii) super-low friction can be achieved with a much lower concentration at a high contact pressure; (iii) GQDs exhibit superior anti-wear performance in comparison to 2D graphitic materials and aqueous glycerol.

The topography and surface features of the worn surfaces lubricated with GQDsaq were examined using an optical microscope, a 3D white-light interferometer, and a SEM. Fig. 4 presents the optical images and 3D morphologies of the wear scar on the steel ball lubricated with different concentrations of GQDsaq (3.5 N, 100 mm/s). Compared to the WSD of the ball (312.5 µm) lubricated with aqueous glycerol (Fig. S3a), the WSDs of the surfaces lubricated with different concentrations of GQDsaq are in the range 118 to 139 µm, indicating a 55–62 % improvement. Moreover, the surface roughnesses of the wear scar on the ball are in the range 20–25 nm, which is lower than the original roughness of the ball (i.e., 40 nm).

The wear scars on the steel ball lubricated under different loads and sliding speeds are presented in Fig. S4. It can be seen in Fig. S4a that the WSD increases with the increase in applied normal load due to the increasing wear with an increase in the load during the running-in period (0.1 wt% GQDsaq, 100 mm/s). However, there is no significant variation in WSD under different sliding speeds, as depicted in Fig. S4b, indicating the excellent lubrication performance of the GQDs in boundary lubrication (0.1 wt% GQDsaq, 3.5 N).

The worn disc surfaces after the 75-min friction test (3.5 N, 100 mm/s) were analyzed using an SEM equipped with EDS to further reveal the lubrication and anti-wear mechanism. The SEM image of the disc (Fig. 5 a) shows that the width of the wear track is ∼120 µm, which agrees with the WSD of the ball under the same contact conditions (Fig. 4c). The maximum wear depth on the disc is about 80 nm (Fig. 5c), indicating excellent anti-wear behavior when compared to the disc surface lubricated with aqueous glycerol (the maximum wear depth is ∼580 nm) (Fig. S3). In addition, the wear track in the center reveals a slight polishing effect and shows hardly any signs of wear, in contrast to the surface lubricated with aqueous glycerol (Fig. S3b). The presence of sample-preparation scratches on the disc’s surface indicates negligible surface wear. In addition, Fig. 5b implies that the tribofilm is formed on the wear track. As shown in Fig. S5, the EDS elemental mapping further confirms the presence of carbon, iron, and oxygen on the wear track, implying that the tribofilm on the worn surface is either due to the adsorption of GQDs or by tribochemical reaction.

This adsorbed film on the disc’s worn surface after the 75-min friction test (3.5 N, 100 mm/s) is further confirmed by the Raman spectroscopy, which shows that the GQDs are adsorbed on the worn surface, forming a carbonaceous layer. As indicated in Fig. 5d, the Raman spectrum of the worn surface (Point 1 in Fig. 5b) shows characteristic D, G, and higher-order 2D bands centered at ∼1340, 1585, and 2700 cm−1 for the graphitic carbon structure [58]. The variation of the ID/IG ratio, which represents the relative intensity of the D band to the G band, reflects the extent of disorder or structural defects in carbon-based materials [66]. It can be seen that the ID/IG ratio increases from 0.98 ± 0.01 (for pristine GQDs) to 1.20 ± 0.03, indicating that the GQDs are partially degraded during the friction test, probably due to the continuous shearing of the adsorbed carbonaceous layer derived from the GQDs in the contact region, which eventually led to the growth of the iron oxide layer [67]. Moreover, the splitting of the D and G bands further suggests the generation of microscopic defects in the graphene [68], which could be attributed to the additional C–OH bond formation [69], as confirmed by the XPS analysis (Fig. 6 a and b). Additionally, a substantial decrease in the intensity of the higher-order bands is attributed to the disruption in the c-axis of the graphitic ordered structure [70], indicating tribo-induced amorphization and structural degradation of the GQDs [71], which is likely to be the consequence of a continuous shearing of the graphene layers in the contact region. The multiple peaks observed below 700 cm−1 are attributed to iron oxide and hydroxide. The peaks observed at ∼211.5 and 284 cm−1 indicate the presence of magnetite (Fe3O4) [72], while the peaks at about 400 and 590 cm−1 confirm the presence of FeOOH and Fe2O3, respectively [73]. An additional peak corresponding to lepidocrocite (γ-FeOOH) was observed at 648 cm−1 [73].

After the 75-min friction test (3.5 N, 100 mm/s) the worn surface of the disc was analyzed with XPS to reveal the interaction between the GQDs and the steel surface. The XPS analysis of the worn surface indicates that the atomic composition in terms of iron, carbon, and oxygen is about 32.7, 64.5, and 2.7 at%, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra on the wear scar on the disk indicate the presence of C–Fe, C–C/C=C, C–O, C=O, and O–C=O groups at binding energies of 283.1, 284.9, 286.3, 288.0, and 288.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 6a). The high-resolution O 1s spectrum reveals that the peak located at a binding energy of 529.6 eV is associated with iron oxide, while the peak centered at 531.5 eV corresponds to either O=C or FeOOH (Fig. 6b). Also, Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 spectra confirm the presence of FeOOH at binding energies of 711.6 eV and 724.3 eV (Fig. 6c) [8,74,75]. In addition, the peak at 533.8 eV corresponds to water molecules that might have been adsorbed from the humid air or formed from the degradation of glycerol. The peak at 532.7 eV corresponds to either the C–O bonds from glycerol or the GQDs. The FeOOH is hydrophilic and acts as an anchor for the hydrogen bond’s interaction with the –OH groups of the GQDs and the water molecules [8,23]. On the other hand, compared to the C 1s spectra of the original GQDs (see Fig. 1f), the concentration of C=O decreased to 6.9 %, while the C=C/C–C concentration increased from 52.1 % to 65.2 %, indicating a reduction of the GQDs due to rubbing [33]. At the same time the concentration of C–O increased from 4.8 % to 17.8 %. Therefore, the density of the oxygen groups is changed because of the sliding. This confirms the dominance of the C–OH or hydroxides on the worn surface. These abundant OH groups on the worn surface and the GQDs positively affect the tribological performance due to the repulsive OH–OH interaction that prevents direct asperity contact [69,76,77]. Interestingly, the C–Fe peak at a binding energy of 283.1 eV confirms the adsorption of GQDs to the native iron surfaces that are exposed during the sliding as a consequence of the wear, as also suggested by M.C. Righi and coworkers [78,79]. In addition, a pronounced metallic Fe or C–Fe bonding peak at 706.7 eV indicates that the chemically adsorbed carbon film protects the native iron surface.

These results confirm that the tribofilm on the worn scar is composed of partially degraded GQDs along with iron oxides and hydroxides, which play a dominant role in achieving excellent tribological performance. Furthermore, the negative ζ potential of the GQDs (i.e., −31.7 mV) provides additional evidence for the interaction of the negatively charged GQDs with the positively charged sites of the worn surface (Fig. S6) [80–83].

To gain further insight into the nanostructure and the thickness of the adsorbed tribofilm formed on the worn surface lubricated with 0.1 wt% GQDsaq (3.5 N, 100 mm/s, 75 min), a cross-sectional lamellar sample with a thickness of a few tens of nanometers was obtained from the disc’s wear track using the FIB-SEM and subsequently analyzed using HRTEM, STEM, EELS, and EDS. The location of the obtained lamella on the wear track is shown in Fig. S7. The cross-sectional HRTEM shows that a tribofilm with a thickness of ∼8.2–35.2 nm is formed, which is clearly visible between the deposited Cr layer and the worn steel surface (Fig. 7 a). The STEM high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) image presented in Fig. 7b reveals that the tribofilm is firmly attached to the worn steel surface by a fuzzy interface of amorphous carbon. Furthermore, it indicates that the GQDs within the carbon-rich tribofilm could not maintain their morphology, implying that the GQDs embedded within the tribofilm are partially degraded compared to the original GQDs (Fig. 1a), indicating the friction-induced structural deformation of the GQDs. This structural degradation of the GQDs is further supported by the increase in the ID/IG ratio observed by the Raman analysis on the worn surface (Fig. 5d), which is probably due to the disruption in the c-axis of the graphitic structure caused by the continuous shearing of graphene layers within the GQDs in contact region [70]. The TEM analysis of the tribofilm (Fig. 7c) reveals that the GQDs somehow maintain their multilayer structure, aligned in the sliding direction. At the same time, most of the GQDs underwent structural degradation, transitioning from a layered configuration to an amorphous state, thereby providing additional evidence for the involvement of the GQDs in the tribochemical reaction during the sliding process. The fast-Fourier-transform (FFT) analysis of Fig. 7c reveals that the crystalline features have a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the lattice fringes of the (11
2
¯
0) plane of graphene in the GQDs [54].

The EELS measurement in the tribofilm area (marked with d in Fig. 7b) reveals the presence of a strong C K-edge at around 292.8 eV and a weak O K-edge and Fe L-edge at about 535.5 eV and 708.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 7d). The C K-edge spectrum (inset Fig. 7d) exhibits a narrow line at 284.9 eV and a slightly broader line at around 292.8 eV, corresponding to the 1s→π* and 1s→σ* transitions, respectively, associated with the sp2 hybridized carbon at the K-edge region [53,84]. The presence of π* further confirms the sp2 hybridized carbon structure inside the tribofilm corresponding to GQDs [85]. The energy difference between π* and σ* is 7.9 eV, which is in good agreement with the value reported for GQDs by Tang et al. [85]. The broad hump at around 325 eV is due to the friction-induced structural damage and the absence of long-range order in the structure of the GQDs [86]. At the same time, the higher-order σ* may refer to the contribution of the carbonyl and carboxyl groups attached to the GQDs [87] or generated consequently due to a tribochemical reaction in an aqueous glycerol environment [8]. The cross-sectional EDS mapping of the tribofilm region (Fig. 7e) further supports the EELS analysis, indicating that the concentration of C is rich in the tribofilm. Additionally, the presence of Fe and O in the tribofilm indicates a tribochemical reaction triggered by the friction process. These observations are consistent with the results obtained using Raman spectroscopy and XPS (Fig. 5d and 6), suggesting that the compacted tribofilm is a tribochemical film composed of an amorphous carbon-based friction product, iron compounds (oxides and hydroxides) with embedded GQDs.

Dispersion stability is one of the prerequisites for nanoparticles to be used as lubricant additives. The results indicate that the GQDs have excellent long-term dispersion stability in aqueous glycerol (Fig. 2) due to their nano-size structure, large surface area, and high edge-to-volume ratio. Furthermore, the interaction between the glycerol and the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the GQDs (Fig. 1d) also facilitates dispersion [82]. This excellent dispersion stability ensures the continuous supply of GQDs in the rubbing interfaces during the sliding process [41]. In contrast, as observed in our previous study [38], graphene sheets with a microscale size in a lateral direction easily squeeze out from the contact areas, resulting in high wear and friction compared to GQDs (Fig. 3a).

The most evident and striking finding from the tribological results presented in Fig. 3 is that the addition of a small concentration of GQDs (i.e., 0.05 wt%) promotes reduced friction and anti-wear performance compared to aqueous glycerol. As shown in Fig. 3b, tribological behavior depends strongly on the concentration, while the wear volume and COF decrease as the concentration of GQDs increases to an optimum value of 0.1 wt%. In comparison, a further increase in the concentration to 0.15 wt% slightly degrades this performance. One of the obvious factors contributing to this improvement is that the size of the GQDs (as shown in Fig. 1a) is considerably smaller (i.e., around 2.5 nm) than the surface roughness of the friction pairs (i.e., 35–40 nm). Therefore, as the GQD concentration increases, a more continuous supply of GQDs is available between the friction surfaces, allowing for greater coverage of the surface irregularities and valleys. Nevertheless, the COF increases when the GQD concentration exceeds the optimal value, possibly due to the aggregation of nanoparticles leading to larger sediments. This leads to a slight deterioration of friction and wear properties, as shown in Fig. 3b and 4d. Our findings agree with a previous report, where Tang et al. [20] suggested that the optimum concentration for improved lubrication performance is 0.1 wt%, and a concentration beyond this level can cause dissolved carbon dots to aggregate, resulting in increased friction.

Likewise, as shown in Fig. 3c, increasing the concentration to 0.1 wt% also improves the MCP up to a maximum value of 316.5 MPa during super-low friction. As the contact pressure increases, the COF increases, and it becomes more difficult to achieve a friction value close to superlubricity, but interestingly, the contact pressure remains above 200 MPa (Fig. 3c), which is rarely observed in self-mated steel contacts using aqueous lubricants [21,26]. This excellent load-carrying performance is because of the formation of a tribofilm with partially degraded GQDs that prevents direct contact between the asperities and improves the tribological performance at a high contact pressure, as confirmed by Raman, XPS, and TEM analysis (Figs. 5, 6 and 7).

The lubrication regime, which is determined by the Lambda parameter (λ), i.e., the ratio of the lubrication film’s thickness and the composite roughness of the tribopairs [88], is crucial to understanding the lubrication mechanism. The film thickness at the super-low friction state was calculated using the Hamrock − Dowson equation (NOTE S6) [88]. The calculated film thickness with 0.1 wt% GQDsaq was found to be 27.6 nm under the applied normal load of 3.5 N and an average relative velocity of 100 mm/s. This smaller film thickness can be ascribed to the higher contact pressure during the super-low friction (Fig. 3c) [33]. In addition, the λ parameter was found to be 0.51, indicating that super-low friction is observed in the boundary-lubrication regime [89]. These findings suggest that this friction reduction is not due to the change in lubrication regime, but should be attributed to boundary-lubrication mechanisms, such as the formation of a protective boundary-lubrication film, ball bearing, and self-healing effects between the tribopairs [20,33,38].

Accordingly, based on our findings, the primary lubrication mechanism for the super-low friction is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 8 . It is well documented that the steel surface becomes positively charged during the running-in phase by emitting low-energy electrons, resulting in a preferential exposure of Fe2+ and Fe3+ on the worn surface [80–83]. As shown by XPS (Fig. 1f), the GQDs have abundant carbonyl and carboxyl groups. Additionally, the XPS analysis on the wear track also confirms the presence of these groups (Fig. 6a and 6b), while Raman spectra on the worn surface verify the adsorption of GQDs (Fig. 5d). These jointly observed phenomena imply that the carbonyl and carboxyl anion are readily adsorbed onto the positively charged worn steel surface via electrostatic interaction (Fig. 8c) [82,83], facilitating the formation of a robust tribofilm consisting of GQDs at the steel interface (Fig. 8b). Fig. S6 further reveals that the GQDs possess more negative ζ potential (i.e., −31.7 mV), which indicates a more significant physical interaction between the negatively charged GQDs and the positively charged sites on the worn surface. M.C. Righi and co-workers [78,79] suggested that the graphene passivates the native iron surfaces exposed during sliding due to wear. The GQDs embedded in the adsorbed tribofilm matrix underwent structural degradation due to exfoliation and interlayer shearing, which ultimately led to a significant reduction in the COF during the running-in phase (Fig. 8b). This process eventually led to the growth of the iron oxide layer [67], as evidenced in the cross-sectional TEM and EDS analysis of the tribofilm presented in Fig. 7e. This chemically adsorbed tribofilm containing GQDs not only protects the surface from wear under extreme-pressure conditions but also improves the frictional performance by suppressing the frictional energy dissipation due to the low shear between and within the tribofilms. At the same time, XPS findings in Fig. 6 suggest the abundance of C-OH and FeOOH on the worn surface in an aqueous lubricating environment, implying that the carbon-rich tribofilm top surface is enriched with the hydroxyl-terminated groups [90]. It is well reported that under the influence of friction, glycerol degrades into water and acidic species, while water molecules dissociate to form H and OH, which are then adsorbed onto the carbon surfaces [69,90,91]. This passivation by hydroxyls terminated groups on the worn surface and GQDs positively affect the tribological performance, due to relatively weak shear strength of the repulsive OH–OH interaction, which reduces direct asperity interaction and facilitates easier sliding of the boundary layers past each other (Fig. 8d) [69,76,77,92]. The surface passivation and the repulsive interaction exerted by the same polarity groups end group (e.g., OH–/OH– or H-/H-) and their positive influence on frictional performance have already been reported several times in the literature [69,90,91,93].

The results above further suggest that the lubrication system containing a tribofilm with partially degraded GQDs improves the load-carrying capacity, resulting in a higher contact pressure during super-low friction (316.5 MPa) compared to a contact lubricated with aqueous glycerol (36.8 MPa). Therefore, the combination of enhanced load-carrying capacity and low shear strength between and within the tribofilms during boundary lubrication enables near superlubricity at a high contact pressure. In particular, the exfoliation and internal shearing of the graphene layers inside GQDs play a pivotal role in reducing the friction during the running-in phase. Meanwhile, the formation of an adsorbed tribofilm containing partially degraded GQDs protects the native iron surface from wear and facilitates the maintenance of super-low friction at a high contact pressure in the boundary-lubrication regime.

The present work highlights the promising tribological properties of GQDs as an additive in aqueous glycerol for steel/steel contacts at a high contact pressure. The addition of a small amount (0.1 wt%) of GQDs to aqueous glycerol significantly shortens the running-in time and reduces the COF to an average value of 0.012, which is approximately equivalent to superlubricity, implying an 88 % improvement over aqueous glycerol. Moreover, the anti-wear performance is improved by 98 %, resulting in super-low friction even at a high contact pressure of 316.5 MPa in the boundary-lubrication regime. The improvement in tribological performance observed is attributed to several factors, including shearing of the graphene layers within the GQDs, exfoliation of the graphene sheets inside GQDs, and OH–OH repulsion between asperities during the running-in phase. Meanwhile, a tribochemical film is formed by the electrostatic adsorption of GQDs on the positively charged worn metal surface, while the GQDs in the tribofilm underwent tribo-induced structural degradation, which protects the surface from wear and facilitates the maintenance of super-low friction under high contact pressure.
